1850.text: dishes listed on a typical dinner menu — the era of the endless feast. Every roast, every relish, every pickle laid out in encyclopedic detail.
could buy a full plate of roast beef at the Astor House, NYC’s grandest hotel. In today’s dollars, roughly $17.

1860.text: is the earliest dated Delmonico’s menu in the collection — the restaurant that invented fine dining in America.
of menus listed prices. Banquets and club dinners dominated — if you had to ask the cost, you shouldn’t be there.

1870.text: menu survives from the 1870s — a decade largely lost to history.

1880.text: of all dish names were in French — the highest of any decade. Peak Francophile dining in America.
appearances of terrapin across the collection. The diamond-back turtle was the Gilded Age’s ultimate luxury, at $2.50 per serving ($80 today).
appearances of plover on menus. Wild game birds — plover, reed birds, snipe, woodcock — were standard fine-dining fare. Most are now protected species.

1890.text: appearances of Apollinaris mineral water — the most-featured brand name in the entire 170-year dataset. The sparkling water of the Gilded Age.
saw the debut of the Club Sandwich (1,334 total appearances), Waldorf Salad (675), and Baked Alaska (327). All still on menus today.
menus collected from this decade. The Gay Nineties saw an explosion of restaurant culture, hotel dining, and ocean-liner service.

1900.text: menus survive from this decade alone — 40% of the entire NYPL collection. The Edwardian era was the golden age of printed menus.
items on a single Waldorf-Astoria menu. Hotel menus were catalogs — guests ordered anything from oysters to Welsh rarebit at any hour.
saw Eggs Benedict, Lobster Thermidor, Crepes Suzette, Chicken à la King, and Chop Suey all debut — dishes that defined American dining for a century.
bought Blue Point oysters at the Marie Antoinette Hotel. Oysters were street food, not luxury — the cheapest protein in New York.

1910.text: appearances of “Ices in Souvenir” — a Waldorf-Astoria signature. Elaborate ice cream molds shaped as flowers or emblems were the era’s Instagram dessert.
was the last year reed birds appeared on any menu. Hunted to near-extinction, the bobolink finally vanished from the American plate.
of new dish names referenced alcohol in the 1910s — a number about to plummet.

1920.text: of dish names referenced alcohol — halved from 9.2% in the 1910s. Prohibition remade the menu.
marked the debut of Canada Dry Ginger Ale on menus. Grape Juice Highball and “Near Beer” filled the void.
saw the first decaffeinated coffee on menus. Sanka went on to 1,292 appearances — one of the most ubiquitous brands in the collection.

1930.text: dishes on a single Plaza Hotel menu in 1933 — the largest menu in the entire collection, compiled during the depths of the Depression.
drop in average dish prices compared to the 1920s. Depression-era menus offered cheaper cuts, simpler preparations, fewer courses.
saw the birth of the Rum Collins and Planter’s Punch. Bartenders reinvented American drinking after Prohibition.

1940.text: of dish names referenced alcohol — triple the Prohibition rate. The cocktail had roared back.
marked the first Caesar Salad on a menu. The Cobb Salad arrived in 1948. American salad culture was being invented.
saw the first Brandy Alexander. Along with the Gin and Tonic (1947) and Daiquiri (1938), the modern cocktail menu took shape.

1950.text: saw the first espresso on an American menu. Cappuccino followed in 1958. The coffee revolution had quietly begun.
marked the debut of the Bloody Mary and the Vodka Martini. Vodka was conquering American bars.
total appearances of Sanka across the collection. On steamships and in hotels, decaf had become a national institution.

1960.text: saw sushi debut on American menus — described as “cold vinegar rice ovals topped with assorted seafoods.” It took 20 more years to go mainstream.
appearances of cottage cheese. Paired with fruit salad, it was the defining “healthy” lunch of the era.

1970.text: of dish names were in French — a surprise resurgence driven by nouvelle cuisine.
menus survive from the 1970s. The collection thins as printed menus gave way to chalkboards and daily specials.

1980.text: saw the first tofu on an American restaurant menu. Health food crossed over from co-ops to restaurants.
saw the simultaneous debut of tiramisu AND bruschetta — both with just 3 appearances. Italian food was about to transform American dining.

1990.text: saw “organic” appear on a menu for the first time. Farm-to-table was still a decade away, but the seeds were planted.
of dish names were in French — the highest since the 1890s. Fine dining’s last French-dominated decade.

2000.text: is the date on the Balthazar menu in the collection — one of very few 21st-century NYC restaurants in the archive.
menus total from the 2000s. The collection was winding down, but captured snapshots of Williamsburg’s Diner and Park Avenue Cafe.

2010.text: menus survive from the 2010s — the collection’s final chapter, closing a 162-year archive.
